reviewer_objection: Is the sf09 public-sector slice publishable at all if it still lacks one exact buyer-side procurement, contract, tender-lot, or page-specific public-assessment companion for a seeded register row?
bounded_response_now: Yes, but as a bounded disclosure-surface and reconstruction-ceiling package rather than a procurement-transparency result. The five-row contribution is that LIMEN can already show four distinct public-sector observability states—official project-page disclosure only, regulator-document depth without buyer-side trace, register-rich disclosure without buyer-side companion, and register-plus-family-level same-buyer procurement continuity—while keeping the missing exact companion as an explicit blocker rather than hiding it.
claim_ceiling_preserved: Yes: the package stays at disclosure asymmetry, observability limits, and evidence architecture rather than denominator, completeness, or procurement-compliance rhetoric.
visualization_or_dashboard_hook: Table 1B reviewer-warning note; sf09 blocker badge; thesis methods/results caveat on reconstruction ceilings.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Keep the blocker explicit in any reuse and spend the next substantive sf09 cycle only on a genuinely different Amsterdam official identifier/public-companion route or a page-specific Helsinki companion.

reviewer_objection: Why keep both Amsterdam and Helsinki in the same panel if Amsterdam is richer on exact-row register disclosure while Helsinki is stronger on buyer-side continuity?
bounded_response_now: Because they answer different reviewer-facing questions. Amsterdam is the register-rich caution exemplar showing that exact row exports, supplier fields, and named governance companions do not by themselves close buyer-side or public-inspectability gaps; Helsinki is the middle-state comparator showing that same-buyer supplier-consistent award metadata can be public while still stopping short of exact row-to-contract proof.
claim_ceiling_preserved: Yes: Amsterdam remains buyer-side unresolved and Helsinki remains family-level linkage only; neither row is promoted into exact procurement reconstruction.
visualization_or_dashboard_hook: Amsterdam caution badge versus Helsinki linkage badge; traceability-ladder contrast inset; Table 1B legend note.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Prefer the Amsterdam-versus-Helsinki contrast sentence from the manuscript bridge whenever prose needs to explain why the two municipal rows are both necessary.

reviewer_objection: Why is the ORION row useful if it is only an official project page and not a register, tender, contract, or enforcement document?
bounded_response_now: Because the public-sector panel needs a disclosure-floor control, not only stronger rows. ORION is the only current row that cleanly shows what an official public-body page can establish—project purpose, governance framing, funding, and pilot language—while still remaining below register, procurement, and regulator-document comparability. That negative space is methodologically useful because it stops downstream prose from flattening all official public-sector evidence into one class.
claim_ceiling_preserved: Yes: ORION stays at public-body-existence and stated-purpose ceiling with no procurement-backed deployment, legality, supplier-normalization, or operational-effectiveness overread.
visualization_or_dashboard_hook: Low-trace floor comparator; project-page-only caution badge; ladder minimum-rung explanation.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Reuse ORION only as the floor row unless a genuinely different official Slovenian procurement, register, technical, or assessment companion appears.

reviewer_objection: Do the two UK ICO rows justify broader claims about procurement opacity, supplier identity, or general public-sector illegality?
bounded_response_now: No. The UK rows are valuable because they provide direct legal-basis, remedy, DPIA, consent, and deployment-timing language from regulator documents, but that authority depth does not substitute for buyer-side procurement reconstruction, supplier trace, or a general prevalence claim about public-sector AI deployments.
claim_ceiling_preserved: Yes: the UK rows remain direct-document governance comparators only, not procurement-proof, supplier-proof, or sector-wide legality evidence.
visualization_or_dashboard_hook: Legal-basis-versus-buyer-trace split; school-biometrics blocker card; regulator-depth legend note.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Keep the UK rows on maintenance status unless one clean official buyer-side procurement, supplier, contract, or public assessment companion appears.

reviewer_objection: Is Helsinki already strong enough to be described as exact contract-linked procurement proof for the parking-chatbot row?
bounded_response_now: No. Helsinki is the strongest current middle-state exemplar because the public package shows same buyer, chatbot modality, IBM-consistent award metadata, and public procurement continuity, but the local evidence still stops at family-level linkage rather than page-specific row-to-contract or row-to-assessment proof.
claim_ceiling_preserved: Yes: Helsinki stays below exact contract-linked, page-specific award, or public-assessment-complete rhetoric.
visualization_or_dashboard_hook: Family-level-linkage badge; middle-state comparator card; frozen-high-ceiling route selector.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Freeze Helsinki below exact linkage unless an already documented official route yields a page-specific contract, tender-lot, award attachment, DPIA, or impact-assessment companion.

reviewer_objection: Does the rich Amsterdam register row plus named governance-companion fields mean LIMEN has already solved public-sector inspectability for municipal AI registers?
bounded_response_now: No. Amsterdam is useful precisely because it separates visible register richness from unresolved inspectability. The row shows exact-row current/history exports, supplier naming, and governance/assessment field presence, but the current captured route set still does not expose a reviewer-checkable buyer-side procurement chain or downloadable public companion document for the same row.
claim_ceiling_preserved: Yes: named governance or assessment fields are not allowed to masquerade as public-document inspectability, procurement resolution, or external validation.
visualization_or_dashboard_hook: Named-assessment-versus-public-inspectability badge; Amsterdam blocker card; municipal-register caution inset.
next_smallest_publishability_move: Attempt only one genuinely different official identifier bridge or static public companion surface already evidenced in local Dutch route packets; do not repeat blocked municipal search or same-shell TenderNed probes.
